speaker: interviewer
text: 17, 1995. Magda, we left off on the last tape with you describing the food that you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the food that you had for dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what else transpired in Auschwitz while you were there? What other incident stands out in your mind that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, it was a terrible in-- incident that it's-- it's in my mind. And I was very lucky on that day because I could have been killed. You know, we had-- we had very little water. And thirst is horrible. Sometimes it can be worse than hunger. And then there were these guards, coming on a truck with these big containers of water, you know, carrying. And they came through our camp. And I remember something-- I was very thirsty, you know? And I saw that still there is humaneness in this guard. Mostly were SS. The Wehrmacht-- there were some Wehrmacht guards, who were-- had some-- who some more humaneness.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this is was a short, little guy. And he was riding that thing. And I speak German, you know? And I went and I ask him if I can have a little water. I was naive, maybe. I was 18 years old. And then, because I did it, you know-- so all the others were coming and begging him for water, you know? And naturally, he-- he started going with his truck-- he-- naturally he didn't give you the water. He started going with his truck. And then, as he was going, you know, water was dripping, you know. That same container was the moving, you know, back and forth, and some water was dripping on the ground. People were running to lick that water, and from the exhaust pipe, the water that just trickled down, you know? And then, he was very upset that any water is-- is-- we are not going to have any water. He said, you want water? And he started you know-- he started to beat some who were close. And then he put his truck. And he was riding and over them and killing them. You know, and was ride-- you know when you want to-- you see in the movies that thing, when you-- you just love to go and-- and crush people, you know? And I never forget-- I have this narrative in my book-- the aftermath. I was praying to God, if you have my book, you are going to find this-- this-- this narrative, in which, you know, on-- I was praying to God to help us. You know, I mean, I was-- I can't tell you what I felt. I felt such a rage and such a helplessness. And-- and I was just hoping that somehow, one day, I will go away from here, you know? I can tell you many stories. But this is-- this is something that I still see those bodies on the ground and this-- this-- this driver, like a mad devil, you know, riding over their bodies. I can never forget that. Now, I have seen many horrible things in Auschwitz besides this, you know? You see what happened to the other side, the people who were taken to the gas chambers. Probably you know the story that they were given towels and-- and-- see, there-- there were four crematory units in Auschwitz. And each was consisting of under one [INAUDIBLE] viaduct and the gas chambers in the crematory ovens, you know? And as I told you, the gas-- the gas chamber had a-- was written bath house. And it has also a peephole. It was administered gas by Sonderkommando, SS Sonderkommando. From-- from-- from above, you know, there was an opening through which gas was administered-- Zyklon B, mixed with cyanide. And then also, there were ducts from outside. You know, they were special. We were Sonderkommando. Everything was Sonderkommando. Who dragged the corpses was the Sonderkommando. Who was putting the gas, those SS-- Sonderkommando. It was just the difference that they were the killers and we had to drag the innocent bodies of the victims. And then-- and then I can see what I can see in Auschwitz. And I never forget the [? carriage ?] of dead, the corpses taken out from the-- from the gas chambers, and then they were hoisted in these-- in-- in these lorries. We called them lorries. And the lorries were pulled by prisoners, taking the bodies to the-- to the crematory ovens. I have some horrible pictures, you know? Those people in the gas chambers died, like brave martyrs of the Hebrew generation, with the greatest Jewish prayer on their mouths-- 'Hear, O Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is One.' I made promises for them. I came-- I never thought I'd survive Auschwitz. I never thought I'd survive three death camps. Because after seven weeks, I was taken out with a transport of 500 women. I still must have looked strong enough. It was a miracle. You know, my uncle was a fencing champion. And I was in competitive fencing. And when I was-- I was sometimes complaining. I am sore. I don't want to do it. He said, you-- I couldn't complain. He said, OK, you have pain? It means you increase your endurance. That's good for you. And I kept on saying, you see, Uncle, you were right. You have to have endurance. You know, you have to tolerate the pain. So I have to tolerate this pain.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a horrible. You know how you drag the corpses, gripped by one hand and the other and drag. And I had-- I see-- I see pictures. I see fellow prisoners bend down, carrying the corpses. I could see people who were shot because they cannot carry the corpses. I have seen a picture from Bergen-Belsen, because there was the same thing there, a transport of men. Until then, you know, in Bergen-Belsen, I always thought that maybe the men have better-- better chance of survival. And I remember when I was in Bergen-Belsen, that picture-- that's my third camp I'm going to talk to you about-- I have see this-- these men dragging corpses. Because we dragged corpses there-- dragging the corpses and bend and a beard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have seen a man who was just like a skeleton, dragging the corpses, dragging. And I see the SS guard hitting him, and hitting him, and hitting him. And he collapses. Then stands up-- and hitting him, hitting him-- drags. And then finally, he just cannot get up anymore, because he's dead. And this was a man of Hungary, a Hungarian men. And they were crying. I said, women, woman, do you have a rutabaga? If I would have had any food-- you know, we had-- if I-- some people who had yet maybe a little bread saved, they would throw it there. And then they would just fight about it, the men, and go and get that piece of bread. I have seen-- you know, it's not like hunger, that you are hungry. But this is starvation that we were exposed to-- starvation, dehydration. That's Auschwitz-- How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Auschwitz for seven weeks. And then I was taken, with 500 women, to the Port of [? Bremen. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going when--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened? They announced?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no idea. We were put into the cattle wagons again. And we were transported. We had no idea what they do with us. We never knew. And I remember then, we arrived to Bremen. And at Bre--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a ride was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a ride was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was maybe-- I can't remember. It was-- it was maybe a day, or something like that. But it was several hours. And we arrived to Bremen. And you know, I can never forget Bremen, the first impression of the arrival. That city was bombed terribly. There were whole streets disappeared. And there were parts of the buildings were sticking out, like-- like tombstones in a cemetery. Then I could see caravans of people moving away from there. It was bombed, day and night, that city. But what did they care?

speaker: interviewer
text: They take-- took us there, to clear the ruins from the city, and drag-- and the corpses from the streets, you know, the aerial victims. And that was-- our-- our-- our food was similar to what we had in Auschwitz, you know? And we had to-- to we-- we were taken to the barracks. In those barracks, we had a mattress, you know, but we were not allowed to do any-- to keep any heating. And you realize, winter caught us there. I never suffered this much cold in my life. We were poorly clad. We-- they gave us wooden Dutch shoes with rags. That's what we were working with, you know, and-- and my-- my dress, you know? And if-- you know, it was highly punishable if you tore up a piece from your dress. And that's what's happened in Auschwitz. I remember, there was a physician with us, you know, in the-- in the column that I was waiting for role call. And she was a little bit, you know-- it was a middle aged woman. And she was always cold, you know? And she took off a piece of-- tore up a piece of her clothing. And the SS woman came, you know? And-- and she said-- like, she has something, you know. Because it was more stuffed. And she pulled this thing out, you know? And you should have seen. She started beating her and beating her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And physicians were not-- actually, were more even persecuted, because, you know, they were intellectuals. And some of these were very uneducated people. And they were jealous. You know, they thought that I'll give it back to them. And-- and then finally, she couldn't stand it anymore. She attacked with-- tried to strangle the SS woman. They took her away and I never have seen her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Auschwitz, what stands in mind also, on a night where it was raining like cats and dogs, we were working with the pails, trying to get the water out. And on the other end, you know, the-- behi-- you know, there was a-- the door, which separated. And the other side, there were the people who saw that we have it much better. And then they broke down the door. And they-- they invaded our place. And then next to the door was a-- one of-- really, she was a little older one, a girl in her 20s. And we became friends, you know? And she-- she tried to survive. And she starting, you know, strangling of the people. And-- but that passed. And then every time there was a rain and we had this, and that happened, she actually became very violent. She be-- she had a mental breakdown. She was taken away. We never have seen here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of these things, you know, they stick in my mind. And in-- in Bremen-- in Bremen, there is something that sticks in my mind that was a miracle. I could have died on that day, but I have two miracles that stick out in my mind, one in Bergen-Belsen and one in Bremen. Now in Bremen, you know, there were bombardments. And we had go in all the holes. There were incendiary bombs. You could have been blown up. Some of my fellow prisoners had horrible frostbite. I came back with-- with frostbites on all my toes and all my fingers. And they were treated, you know, and eventually healed. It took me a year of treatment.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was the day of atonement-- [INAUDIBLE] one of our biggest holidays. And I was so far didn't get any illness, you know? But I woke up in the middle of the night in my barrack. And I felt a terrible sore throat. And I knew that it must be strep throat. And I knew I was feverish. I was dizzy. And there was an infirmary, you know, in the hall. You have to be half dead to get something from there. And if you stayed long enough, it was dangerous, because they would take you to Auschwitz, you know? And-- and I-- I was wobbling, you know? There were bunks, you know-- three bunks. How do you call-- tiers, you know? And I was on the third, because I was the youngest. So they said I can climb better. And I came down from the tier. And I was very sick. And I went into the infirmary. They threw me out. I am not sick enough to get anything. I went to back to my barrack. I traded my bread for a little soup so I can-- I could hardly swallow the soup, even. And the next morning, we had to go out. It was cold. And I had to work on the streets with fever and with the strep throat. I thought I'm going to die. I begged the guard to let me stay in the barracks. No, I am not sick enough to stay in. So we went. And you know, the interesting thing there-- this reminds me of a Hanukkah and the miracle. We worked at the site of a-- of a-- of a bombed preserve factory. And as I was digging, I found two little jars of jelly. And one of my fellow prisoners, you know, found a kind of a coat-- you know what it means-- which had a pocket. I mean, this was very unusual. It's like an overcoat, a light one. But you know what it meant to have something like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I thought, I have to have this jelly. I can't eat anything. I put these two jellies in my pocket. And I was praying to God to send the bombers so I can go somewhere inside, in a shelter. And see, in-- in Germany, there were two kinds of shelters. There were the-- some of several stories, and there were the underground ones. The underground ones was the worst, because it's a-- if it hits you a direct hit, you die-- perish in there. And I took my two little jars. And I was hiding it in my pocket. And I didn't know how I am going to take that in the camps, because they were checking you. And if they found food, you were beaten up. You went to the punishment cell. And nevertheless, I couldn't resist not-- these two little jars I was hiding. It was a terrific bombardment. We hear the-- the sirens, so we go to the shelter. And we went to the underground shelter. And this was a-- close by was a railroad station, the worst place you can be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you never-- I hope you never will hear the sound of this, the whistling sound of the bombs and the big detonations. And suddenly, you know, we had-- these bunkers had concrete doors. There was a German guard, a Wehrmacht, who said, where the Jews are never hits the bombs. So and he was the only one who stayed with us. Others-- you know what they did? They locked us there and they went into the safer thing. So if we are hit, fine. We can perish there. So was a-- suddenly, you know, I hear a big detonation and the whistle. And the big concrete door opens by itself. The light goes out and the bunker starts shaking. And I said, God, if I have to die, just let me be fast. I don't want to be an invalid and I don't want to end up in the gas chambers. And suddenly, it was-- stopped, you know, the whole thing. And when we got out of the bunker, it was horrible. You know, houses were burning.

speaker: interviewee
text: People stand-- people ran out of the houses, burning with their clothes. It was a-- it was a chaos. We went to our camps. Our camp was leveled. If I would have been in barrack, I would have died. We went back to the camps. I had still the terrible sore throat. I had the fever. And then when night came, you know, they didn't check us, because they put us in another-- other-- another barracks, other camp. And it was a chaos and transportation problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, at night, you know, I pulled out my little jars. And I had to eat those two jars, because I couldn't risk. And I thought, how I am going to take these out in the morning without being checked, you know, the jars. If you put it in the mattress, they check your mattress every day, to see if you have paper or pencil. It was gravely punished. And through the night, I-- in the-- I gorged myself with those two things. And you know, in the morning, my whole sore throat-- everything was gone. I could never understand, you know? That was a miracle. Those two little jars, you know, lasted for the night and made me hap-- made me healthy in the morning. So we stayed there through winter. And it was a terrible thing. You know, by the end of winter-- but at least, the one thing, you were working on the streets. And if you had a bad guard, he didn't let you do anything. But if you had a Wehrmacht, one of the Wehrmacht-- very few, maybe two that I can count-- maybe they let you raid the garbage can if you wanted it, you know? I mean, it was-- it was a-- maybe you can get some scraps of food from somewhere, you know? But you had to be careful, because it depended what guard you had. And were heavy labor-- we had to lift heavy stones, heavy pipes, bricks. And one thing sticks out. At one point, there was a heavy thing we had to carry. And we are women. Even for men, it's heavy. And I went to the guard. And I said, we have a problem. I always did these things, hoping for the best. We have problems. We need help. He said, oh, you need help? I'm going to help you. He came. He said, I am going to count until three. If-- if you don't lift it, I-- I shoot all of you. And we did it. You know, they say that you did the impossible when you have to do it. We did it. That's something sticks out from Bremen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, at the end of the winter, we were all already used up, I call that-- used up. We were-- lost a great deal of weight. It looked like we were not going to be useful anymore. So our destination was Bergen-Belsen, the death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anybody that you knew in Bremen? Were you with any friends, with any relative?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with some-- I was with two-- two very-- two people whom we know very well, from the city. And I was all along with that schoolmate I was talking about. She was with me in Auschwitz, in Bremen, and in Bergen-Belsen too.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bremen, did anybody try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, really, nobody tried to escape. Because you were afraid that you are going to be denounced. Where would you go? What if-- you know, it's in a-- in a city. Where would you go?

speaker: interviewer
text: During this whole period of time, Auschwitz and Bremen, did you have a menstrual period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is something I have to tell you, that we all lost our periods. We lost it for a year. I didn't have my period until I was liberated, later on. And I said that maybe they administered us something in our food. I don't know. But you see what happened in Auschwitz-- we didn't get any napkins, nothing. So it was a terrible situation, a terrible-- they didn't want that, so that we lost our periods. And this is-- I don't know it's doing to-- due to the stress, or they administered something. Because we-- I wasn't the only one, you know. We lost our periods. And it lasted. It lasted for a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz, or in Bremen, did any woman give birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now you are-- you are coming to a very, very terrible subject, you know. Because you see, there were women who were pregnant. But at the time that they entered, they-- it wasn't visible. And when it was visible-- I know we had a-- a pregnant woman among us. You tried to hide that person as much as you can. And then we-- it's known. It's known. If she was discovered, she would go to the gas chambers, she and the-- so if a physician had access-- I don't know. There were-- we heard that in some camps a physician had access. They would inject the mother so the baby dies, so they save the mother. And if a woman gave birth to the baby, they would kill-- they would inject the baby. Because otherwise, the mother would go toge-- I mean, there were some horrible things. It was a horrible thing to be pregnant in Asuchwitz, horrible thing. But these things happened. I know for sure, that-- that there were-- because we-- we heard these-- there were not only rumors that if a physician, you know, for somehow got access, you know, to work-- I don't know-- in-- in-- to-- to-- to work, and had access to medication, she would do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of any of the commandants in Bremen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bremen, no. But Rudolf Ferdinand Hoss was in Auschwitz. And I remember Kramer in Bergen-Belsen. We called him the beast of Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in-- in Bremen, you don't remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bremen-- see, in Bremen, I can't remember any of the commandants. You see, what they did-- they really hauled us out from Auschwitz. And then they were paid for our labors, you know? We didn't get an-- we were the slave laborers for these construction companies, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Bremen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in Bremen through the whole winter and fall of 1944. And we-- by end of March, they took us from Bremen. Because I think they-- they-- we didn't look any more good for work. We were weakened, really. And then we had to march on foot for 30 kilometers. I tell you, I call that a death march. Many of us didn't make it. Some were shot. Others collapsed. I could tell you a lot about this death march. And then, finally, some of us who arrived arrived to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about the death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, see, the death march-- we were, first of all, poorly clad. And-- and our guards were very nicely dressed up with boots, you know? And we-- we had-- I remember that during the death march, we had a stop. And we-- that sticks into my memory. It was like a hut of some sort. And we were locked in there. And we were not allowed to get out. And if we had-- if we had to toilet is we had to make it there. And I remember, you know, we-- we didn't get any utensils. So we had a kind of a-- you know, like the soldiers have a metal thing and you can drink your soup from there. And-- and we had to use that for other purpose. And then we heard-- there was a very bad SS woman, who was with us-- very-- one of the-- some of the worst, who would strip you in the middle of the winter, from-- if you had a rag around you, because you work faster if you are cold. And we heard that she tried to put that hut on fire. But she didn't manage to. She was a psychopath. They were psychopaths. See, many of these SS guards-- I'm telling you, they were psychopaths. They had a license to kill and to torture you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on this march? How many days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think at least two days, or three. We were marching. And then-- and-- and we got weaker and weaker. And finally, we had-- it was 30 kilometers, you know? And we arrived to Bergen-Belsen in the middle of the night. It was pitch dark. And we got into the camps. I felt a terrible smell of death. I was familiar with the odor of death. And I thought that probably they are going to shoot us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like a craze, you know? Some people formed columns. And some people were pushed into some barracks by these guards. And there were hardly any place. The people were screaming outside. I was at the end of the column. Finally, there was no place-- no way that you could put more people in there. And they said, well, rest of us who are out there, we had to-- we had to sleep right on the ground, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when morning came, you know, the ground of Bergen-Belsen was littered with corpses, of all stages of decay. And I was seeing where we slept, next to the corpses. In front of me there were some pyramid of dead, where-- where the bodies were stripped of clothing. We called the Pyramid of Dead. And they were hoisted up, you know, in a shape of a pyramid-- and that horrible corpses, of all stages of decay. So we-- all the time in Bergen-Belsen, three weeks was your life expectancy there. Because you were taken when you were weakened. And all this time, I slept next to the corpses. I dragged the corpses. And I dug the pits. That was the work, until you one day collapse and you die. They gradually didn't give us any food-- no solid food, no-- no water. And your tongues fill up with wounds so you can hardly swallow. I mean, I can see that you can go crazy by lack of water. And that lasted for-- I mean, in the-- now there was a happening there, which in Bergen-Belsen I can never forget. I can never explain. I think really God wanted to save me for a purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was for two-- it was the second week, towards the end of the second week. And they said, well, you are not going to sleep anymore on the ground because you are going to get in the barracks. So as we-- I-- I could see, we are marching for a while. We are-- we approached the barrack. And I could see-- that is in my mind. I will never forget. I could see a woman thrown out like a piece of garbage from the barracks. And she had the typhus, you know? And her-- her eyes were kind of glossy. It was a horrible-- she had the kiss of death, I say.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I realized-- we realized what they do to us. They brought us into the typhus barracks, because the typhus was-- we were infested with lice. And the-- the typhus is transmitted through the sick lice. And this little SS woman, with the gun on the side, was pushing and pushing us in. And you know, there was something that I can never understand. You know, I never lost my faith in God. I thought, if somebody can save me from that, it's only God.

speaker: interviewer
text: She picked me. I looked pretty bad, you know? And she does this to me-- you. And I go there. And she said, you go up there. And I see, on the third bunk, a women dying of typhus-- infested. To me, it spelled out death. What came over with me, I don't know. It was a guidance from above. I started running across the barrack, straight. And it were many people in the barrack. I was-- I never-- I'm never pushy like that. But I-- I wanted to get to the end of the barracks. And she was coming after me. But you know what happened? My maiden name is Moses. Some other guard from outside pushed in a big crowd. And she was absolutely caught. She couldn't follow me. She could not-- she was immobilized. And I went to the end of the barrack. It was an open window. And I jumped out. And I was hiding through the night.
